Deafblindness communication invention

A world you can feel.A voice the room can hear.

GLOVE: Guided Language Over Vibrational Encoding

GLOVE is an AI tactile communication ecosystem for deafblind people. It is designed to translate voice, text, photos, camera scenes, writing and object information into safe tactile language through a smart glove — then translate the user’s signs, taps, writing and gestures back into spoken voice.

See the invention systemHow the glove worksContact Cornelius

Two-way communication

World to user. User back to world.

Tactile language

Safe touch patterns, not random buzzing.

AI simplification

Voice, text, images and scenes become clear meaning.

Grows with the user

Starts with core words and expands over time.

Product visuals near the top

The full invention, visible immediately.

All product images are embedded inside this single index.html file, scaled for phones, tablets and desktop screens, and clickable for a larger view.

How the glove works

Information enters the system. Meaning reaches the hand.

The glove is the user’s communication surface. It receives tactile language and also detects the user’s response.

1

Capture

Voice, text, camera scenes, photos, writing, keyboard input, object tags or user signs enter the system.

2

Understand

The AI identifies the important meaning: person, object, action, place, feeling, question, warning or instruction.

3

Simplify

Long speech becomes simple meaning. “Would you like water now?” becomes question + want + water + now.

4

Encode

The tactile language engine maps meaning to hand location, rhythm, pressure, pulse strength, duration and sequence.

5

Deliver

The glove sends gentle, learnable patterns across the palm, fingers and wrist so the user can feel the message.

6

Respond

The user signs, taps, writes or types. The system translates the response into voice or text for other people.

The core invention

A tactile language built for the hand.

GLOVE should not be random vibration. It is a structured touch language. Different areas, rhythms and sequences carry different meanings that the user can learn over time.

  • Location tells the category: person, object, action, feeling, question or warning.
  • Rhythm gives shape to the word or phrase.
  • Pressure and intensity can show urgency or importance.
  • Duration and repetition help separate similar words and confirm meaning.

Example hand map

Meaning can be organised by touch zone.

ThumbPeople, self, family, identity.
Index fingerObjects and naming.
Middle fingerActions and movement.
Ring fingerFeelings and body needs.
Little fingerQuestions, choices, yes and no.
PalmCore words: help, stop, more, pain, food, drink.
WristAlerts, routines, time and environmental warnings.
Whole handConfirmation, emergency and high-priority messages.

Learning from birth

A deafblind baby needs meaning before complicated language.

The learning path begins with safe touch, real objects and repetition. The aim is not to overwhelm the child. The aim is to build a bridge from experience to meaning.

1

Comfort and trust

The child first learns that the device is safe, gentle, removable and connected to calm human interaction.

2

Cause and effect

A pattern happens before a real event: drink, food, mum, sleep, play, stop or more.

3

Object connection

The child holds a cup while the teacher says “cup” and the glove sends the cup pattern.

4

Choice-making

The user feels two options, touches one, and learns that communication can change what happens next.

5

Needs and feelings

Early words include help, pain, toilet, hungry, thirsty, tired, scared, happy, yes and no.

6

People and routines

Daily life becomes more predictable: mum, dad, teacher, home, school, bath, bed, food time and play time.

7

Letters and writing

The smart pen and tactile keyboard introduce letters, names, spelling, tracing and written messages.

8

Conversation

The system grows from single words to short phrases, then longer communication when the user is ready.

Product ecosystem

Each invention solves one part of daily communication.

Together, the devices create one loop: the world becomes tactile language, and the user’s response becomes voice or text.

System component

AI Smart Glove

The central device. It delivers tactile language to the hand and detects the user’s signs, taps, pressure and gestures. It is both receiver and sender.

  • Haptic points across palm, fingers and wrist.
  • Pressure, motion and finger-position sensors.
  • Gesture and sign recognition for two-way communication.
  • Adjustable intensity, quiet mode and personal vocabulary.
View product visual

System component

AI Camera Glasses

The glasses act as the world-reader. They identify text, objects, people, signs, photos, hazards and scenes, then send simplified meaning to the glove.

  • Reads signs, menus, labels, printed text and screens.
  • Recognises everyday objects, doors, obstacles and surroundings.
  • Turns photos into tactile meaning.
  • Sends useful meaning, not overwhelming detail.
View product visual

System component

Mobile App / AI Brain

The app connects every device, manages translation and controls learning levels, vocabulary, settings, photos, signs, safety and progress.

  • Voice-to-touch, text-to-touch and photo-to-touch translation.
  • Learning modes for baby, child, conversation, emergency and quiet use.
  • Caregiver and teacher controls for daily routines.
  • Private profiles for vocabulary and sensitivity.
View product visual

System component

Charging / Connection Hub

The hub keeps the full system together. It charges, stores, pairs and protects the glove, glasses, voice responder, smart pen and keyboard.

  • Multi-device charging and automatic pairing.
  • Travel-ready storage for school, home and daily routines.
  • Status lights for ready, charging, sync and attention.
  • A single organised base for the entire ecosystem.
View product visual

System component

Smart Pen

The pen turns writing, tracing and spelling into communication. It helps the user learn letters, practise movement and send written messages.

  • Recognises letters, numbers, names, simple words and shapes.
  • Confirms writing through tactile feedback in the glove.
  • Supports tracing, school learning, spelling and homework.
  • Can convert written output into voice.
View product visual

System component

Tactile Keyboard

The keyboard gives an advanced communication route for typing, education and independent expression.

  • Raised tactile keys for touch-based typing.
  • Typed words can be spoken aloud by the AI voice responder.
  • Useful for school, work, messages and longer answers.
  • Glove confirmation helps the user know what has been entered.
View product visual

System component

Photo-to-Tactile Translation

A photo becomes touch-based meaning. The AI identifies key objects, people, actions, places and feelings, then sends a simplified tactile description.

  • A dog photo can become animal + dog + sitting.
  • A family photo can become mum + dad + child + happy.
  • A warning sign can become warning + wet floor + careful.
  • Useful for learning, memories, safety and education.
View product visual

System component

Complete Ecosystem Bundle

The full bundle works as one assistive technology system, not separate gadgets. Each device supports the same tactile language.

  • Camera and voice inputs become touch.
  • User signs, taps, writing and typing become voice or text.
  • The system grows from early childhood into advanced communication.
  • Designed for families, schools, carers, researchers and prototype partners.
View product visual

Day-to-day life

How the ecosystem helps in real situations.

  • At breakfast: the parent says “Do you want more?” and the glove sends question + want + more. The child taps yes or no.
  • At school: the teacher selects book, writing, finished or help so routines become predictable.
  • Outside: the glasses detect steps, doors, vehicles, people, objects or warning signs and send only the useful alert.
  • With photos: an image of a dog, family member or place becomes tactile meaning the user can feel.
  • When expressing needs: signs and taps become spoken phrases such as “I need help,” “I am in pain,” or “I want a drink.”

Communication loop

World to user. User back to world.

Voice, text, photos and camera scenes are simplified into meaning, then delivered as tactile language through the glove. The user can answer through signs, taps, writing or typing. The AI voice responder speaks the response aloud.

The system is an invention concept intended for prototype development, specialist review, safeguarding review and real-world testing with deafblind experts, families and educators.

Why it matters

The benefit is not just technology. The benefit is connection.

Earlier language access

Creates a touch-based route into meaning for children who cannot rely on sight or sound.

Less isolation

Turns the world into information the user can receive, understand and respond to.

Choice and control

Yes, no, stop, more, help and pain can become practical communication, not guesswork.

Family communication

Parents and carers get a clearer way to build repeated, meaningful routines.

Education support

Writing, spelling, object learning, classroom routines and vocabulary can connect through touch.

Safer daily life

Camera alerts, object recognition and emergency phrases can support movement and independence.

Two-way expression

The user’s signs, taps and writing can become spoken voice for others to hear.

Personalised growth

The system can begin with simple core meanings and expand as the user learns.

Prototype roadmap

Build the smallest version that proves the language can be learned.

  • Prototype 1: a soft hand pad or glove with 8–12 haptic points and 20 core words.
  • Prototype 2: phone app control, pattern editor, intensity settings and training mode.
  • Prototype 3: speech-to-simple-meaning and text-to-tactile translation.
  • Prototype 4: user tap/sign response translated into voice output.
  • Prototype 5: professional testing with deafblind specialists, families, educators and ethics review.

Safety principles

Designed to support people, not replace people.

GLOVE must support human touch, family connection and specialist education. It should not force constant signals or replace hand-under-hand communication, real objects, tactile signing or professional support.

  • Comfort mode, quiet mode and user-controlled pause.
  • Safe intensity, safe materials and easy cleaning.
  • Privacy-first handling of voice, camera and learning data.
  • Professional review before use with babies or children.

Inventor concept by Cornelius Aurelius

GLOVE is designed to open another doorway into communication.

A tactile language ecosystem for deafblindness: world to touch, user back to voice.
